speaker: Unknown
en: You decided to join Twitter recently, put out your first tweet.
zh: 你最近决定加入 Twitter，发出了你的第一条推文。

speaker: Unknown
en: Marc Andreessen quote tweeted it and said, "This is the best AI CEO nobody knows."
zh: Marc Andreessen 引用了这条推文并说道，“这是没人知道的最好的 AI CEO。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Our best work is done alone and quietly.
zh: 我们最好的工作是独自安静地完成的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Every minute you're writing something for public consumption, you're not focusing your very limited time that you have on your customers and your product.
zh: 每一分钟你都在为公共消费写东西，你就不是把你非常有限的时间集中在你的客户和产品上。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're building a lot of the future that we're going to be living in.
zh: 你正在构建我们要生活在其中的很多未来。

speaker: Unknown
en: What does the next couple years look like?
zh: 接下来的几年会是什么样子？

speaker: Unknown
en: Us solving some of these impossible problems like cancer are directly going to be related to this AI boom.
zh: 我们解决像癌症这样的一些不可能的问题，将直接与这次 AI 繁荣有关。

speaker: Unknown
en: Net suffering in humanity overall should go down significantly.
zh: 人类的整体净苦难应该会显著下降。

speaker: Unknown
en: A thread that has emerged on this podcast is that AI is coming just in time to save us.
zh: 这个播客中出现的一个主题是，AI 正好及时出现来拯救我们。

speaker: Unknown
en: The real impact of AI in the next 5 to 10 years really is going to be in farming, mining, construction.
zh: AI 在未来 5 到 10 年的真正影响将真正是在农业、采矿和建筑领域。

speaker: Unknown
en: These industries, they need autonomy and it couldn't come soon enough.
zh: 这些行业，它们需要自主性，而且来得越快越好。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you look at farmers, the average age of a farmer is in their late 50s.
zh: 如果你看看农民，农民的平均年龄是在 50 多岁后期。

speaker: Unknown
en: What does that mean in 10 years from now?
zh: 从现在起 10 年后那意味着什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a lot of anxiety about what AI is going to do to the world.
zh: 对于 AI 将对世界做什么有很多焦虑。

speaker: Unknown
en: The core root of fear is misunderstanding.
zh: 恐惧的核心根源是误解。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you at home are very anxious about AI, the best thing that you can do is spend time to understand and you will quickly see the limitations.
zh: 如果你在家对 AI 感到非常焦虑，你能做的最好的事情就是花时间去理解，你很快就会看到局限性。

speaker: Unknown
en: Get to know it, then actively make the technology be used for good.
zh: 了解它，然后主动让技术被用于善途。

speaker: Unknown
en: Today, my guest is Qasar Younis, co-founder and CEO of Applied Intuition.
zh: 今天，我的嘉宾是 Qasar Younis，Applied Intuition 的联合创始人兼 CEO。

speaker: Unknown
en: You've probably never heard of Qasar or Applied Intuition.
zh: 你可能从未听说过 Qasar 或 Applied Intuition。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is the most important under the radar AI company and CEO that I've ever come across.
zh: 这是我见过的最重要的隐形 AI 公司和 CEO。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a $15 billion company that has been growing quietly over the last decade.
zh: 这是一家价值 150 亿美元的公司，在过去十年中一直在悄悄增长。

speaker: Unknown
en: What they do is they add AI to vehicles like cars, tractors, planes, submarines, mining rigs, and a lot more.
zh: 他们所做的是给车辆添加 AI，如汽车、拖拉机、飞机、潜艇、采矿钻机等等。

speaker: Unknown
en: 18 out of the top 20 automakers are customers, as well as the biggest global construction, mining, and trucking companies.
zh: 前 20 大汽车制造商中有 18 家是客户，以及最大的全球建筑、采矿和运输公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: Also the Department of Defense.
zh: 还有国防部。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're basically Waymo or Tesla, but without the hardware.
zh: 他们基本上就是 Waymo 或 Tesla，但是没有硬件。

speaker: Unknown
en: Qasar himself was born on a farm in Pakistan, grew up in Detroit, started his career as an engineer at GM, and then at Bosch.
zh: Qasar 本人出生在巴基斯坦的一个农场，在底特律长大，职业生涯开始时是 GM 和 Bosch 的工程师。

speaker: Unknown
en: He then went on to start a couple companies before starting Applied Intuition.
zh: 然后他继续创办了几家公司，之后创办了 Applied Intuition。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love everything about this episode and I am so excited to bring it to you.
zh: 我喜欢这一集的一切，我很兴奋把它带给你。

speaker: Unknown
en: Don't forget to check out lennysproductpass.com for an incredible set of deals available exclusively to Lenny's newsletter subscribers.
zh: 不要忘记查看 lennysproductpass.com，那里有一套专门提供给 Lenny 的通讯订阅者的惊人优惠。

speaker: Unknown
en: Let's get into it after a short word from our wonderful sponsors.
zh: 让我们在来自我们优秀赞助商的简短广告后进入正题。

speaker: Unknown
en: This episode is brought to you by Omni.
zh: 这一集由 Omni 赞助。

speaker: Unknown
en: Many product teams today are in the process of debating how to ship AI analytics.
zh: 如今许多产品团队正在辩论如何交付 AI 分析。

speaker: Unknown
en: The hard part is obvious.
zh: 困难的部分是显而易见的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Having an LLM guess at SQL in production is a huge mess and just a bad idea.
zh: 在生产环境中让 LLM 猜测 SQL 是一团糟，也是一个坏主意。

speaker: Unknown
en: Omni takes a different approach.
zh: Omni 采取了不同的方法。

speaker: Unknown
en: They have a semantic layer built in so that when you embed their analytics, the AI actually knows your business definitions, not just your raw tables.
zh: 他们内置了一个语义层，所以当你嵌入他们的分析时，AI 实际上知道你的业务定义，而不仅仅是你的原始表。

speaker: Unknown
en: You can test queries, validate the reasoning, and lock down permissions before anything hits production.
zh: 你可以在任何东西进入生产环境之前测试查询、验证推理并锁定权限。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you want AI analytics in your product without building the whole stack from scratch, check out omni.co/lenny for a free three-week trial.
zh: 如果你想要你的产品中有 AI 分析，而不需要从头构建整个堆栈，请访问 omni.co/lenny 进行免费的三周试用。

speaker: Unknown
en: Companies like Perplexity, DBT, and BuzzFeed use Omni to ship analytics their customers can trust.
zh: 像 Perplexity、DBT 和 BuzzFeed 这样的公司使用 Omni 来交付他们的客户可以信任的分析。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's O-M-N-I.co/lenny.
zh: 那是 O-M-N-I.co/lenny。

speaker: Unknown
en: My podcast guests and I love talking about craft, and taste, and agency, and product market fit.
zh: 我的播客嘉宾和我喜欢谈论技艺、品味、智能体和产品市场契合度。

speaker: Unknown
en: You know what we don't love talking about?
zh: 你知道我们不喜欢谈论什么吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: SOC 2.
zh: SOC 2。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's where Vanta comes in.
zh: 这就是 Vanta 发挥作用的地方。

speaker: Unknown
en: Vanta helps companies of all sizes get compliant fast and stay that way with industry-leading AI, automation, and continuous monitoring.
zh: Vanta 帮助所有规模的公司快速合规并保持合规，使用行业领先的 AI、自动化和持续监控。

speaker: Unknown
en: Whether you're a startup tackling your first SOC 2, or ISO 27001, or an enterprise managing vendor risk, Vanta's trust management platform makes it quicker, easier, and more scalable.
zh: 无论你是正在处理第一个 SOC 2 或 ISO 27001 的初创公司，还是管理供应商风险的企业，Vanta 的信任管理平台都使其更快、更容易且更具可扩展性。

speaker: Unknown
en: Vanta also helps you complete security questionnaires up to five times faster so that you can win bigger deals sooner.
zh: Vanta 还可以帮助你快 5 倍完成安全问卷，以便你能够更快赢得更大的交易。

speaker: Unknown
en: The result?
zh: 结果呢？

speaker: Unknown
en: According to a recent IDC study, Vanta customers slashed over $500,000 a year and are three times more productive.
zh: 根据最近的一项 IDC 研究，Vanta 客户每年节省超过 50 万美元，并且生产力提高了三倍。

speaker: Unknown
en: Establishing trust isn't optional.
zh: 建立信任不是可选项。

speaker: Unknown
en: Vanta makes it automatic.
zh: Vanta 让这变得自动化。

speaker: Unknown
en: Get $1,000 off at vanta.com/lenny.
zh: 在 vanta.com/lenny 可享受 1000 美元的优惠。

speaker: Unknown
en: Qasar, thank you so much for being here.
zh: Qasar，非常感谢你来到这里。

speaker: Unknown
en: Welcome to the podcast.
zh: 欢迎来到播客。

speaker: Unknown
en: Thanks for having me.
zh: 谢谢你的邀请。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're basically building a lot of the future that we're going to be living in and people may not even realize this.
zh: 你基本上正在构建我们将要生活在其中的大量未来，人们甚至可能没有意识到这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's two sides to this.
zh: 这有两个方面。

speaker: Unknown
en: On the one side, let me ask you this question.
zh: 一方面，让我问你这个问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: If things go really well, what does the next couple years look like for people with the emergence of AI with physical AI?
zh: 如果事情进展得非常顺利，随着物理 AI 的出现，未来几年对人们来说会是什么样子？

speaker: Unknown
en: What's a vision of the future?
zh: 未来的愿景是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Let me take the broader AI point and then the more specific one on physical AI.
zh: 让我先从更广泛的 AI 角度来讲，然后再讲物理 AI 这个更具体的角度。

speaker: Unknown
en: Macro, think about this like the Industrial Revolution.
zh: 宏观上，把这想象成工业革命。

speaker: Unknown
en: So if you are sitting, let's say in the late 1800s, we can focus on a lot of bad things that happen because of Industrial Revolution.
zh: 所以如果你是坐在，比如说 19 世纪末，我们可以专注于因工业革命而发生的大量坏事。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have child labor and you have monopolies emerging and you have abuse of... Wars end up happening.
zh: 你有童工，你有垄断企业出现，你有对……的滥用，战争最终爆发。

speaker: Unknown
en: But there's also, it's an almost unimaginable present without the values and without the kind of benefits we got of the Industrial Revolution, which is broader access to healthcare like we've never seen before.
zh: 但也有，如果没有我们从工业革命中获得的价值观和那种好处，那就是一个几乎无法想象的现在，这种好处是以前从未见过的更广泛的医疗保健机会。 [[65] ] 获得商品，物质商品，我们视为理所当然的事情，比如给家里供暖和制冷。

speaker: Unknown
en: Access to goods, material goods, things like we take for granted, like heating and cooling your home.
zh: 获取商品，实物商品，那些我们视为理所当然的东西，比如给家里供暖和制冷。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's this great YouTube channel that focuses on POW letters from Germans who are seeing America in the early '40s, and they're writing letters back to Germany about what they're seeing as they're basically prisoners of war.
zh: 有一个很棒的 YouTube 频道，专注于德国战俘在 40 年代初看到美国时写的信，他们基本上是在作为战俘的时候写信回德国描述他们的所见所闻。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they're kind of blown away that the towns that they roll by in these trains as they're going to their POW camps are all lit up or that there's cars everywhere.
zh: 他们有点震惊，因为在去往战俘营的火车上经过的那些城镇都灯火通明，或者到处都是汽车。

speaker: Unknown
en: 80% of German towns in World War II did not have electricity.
zh: 第二次世界大战期间 80% 的德国城镇没有电力。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's kind of a mind bending kind of thing because we just assume all this stuff, all this technology is equally distributed.
zh: 这有点令人难以置信，因为我们只是假设所有这些东西，所有这些技术都是平均分布的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the positive version is these things that we, let's say folks who are wealthy or folks who have access to technology, these things everybody has access to.
zh: 所以积极的版本是，这些东西，比如说那些拥有财富或有机会接触技术的人，这些东西每个人都有机会接触到。

speaker: Unknown
en: The fact like simply having somebody who's a coach to you and having that coach very specifically to you, not a generic ChatGPT that's giving fairly generic answers, that's a very powerful thing.
zh: 事实上，仅仅拥有一个辅导你的教练，并且那个教练是专门针对你的，而不是一个给出相当通用答案的通用 ChatGPT，这是一件非常强大的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think us solving some of these impossible problems like cancer are directly going to be related to this AI boom.
zh: 我认为我们解决一些不可能的问题，比如癌症，将直接与这次 AI 繁荣有关。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think net suffering in humanity, I think just like the Industrial Revolution overall should go down and should go down significantly.
zh: 所以我认为人类的净苦难，我认为就像工业革命整体一样，应该会下降，并且应该大幅下降。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I'm a fundamental optimist in that view that technology will bring that positivity.
zh: 在那个观点上，我是一个根本的乐观主义者，我认为技术将带来这种积极性。

speaker: Unknown
en: In physical AI specifically, again, when you have things like you have your own car and you have the ability, you have your limbs, and you have your senses, and you can drive, you take these kind of things for granted.
zh: 具体在物理 AI 方面，同样，当你拥有像你自己的汽车这样的东西，并且你有能力，你有你的四肢，你有你的感官，你可以驾驶，你会把这些事情视为理所当然。

speaker: Unknown
en: You jump in your car and you go to the store.
zh: 你跳上你的车去商店。

speaker: Unknown
en: For somebody who maybe is disabled or somebody who doesn't have the money to afford a vehicle, access to mobility that's nearly free or is free is a big deal.
zh: 对于可能残疾的人来说，或者对于没有钱买得起车辆的人来说，获得几乎免费或免费的出行能力是一件大事。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that simple example of making self-driving cars free for everybody and how that would change the planet, you live in Rwanda and you are two hours from the nearest hospital, that matters.
zh: 这个简单的例子，让自动驾驶汽车对每个人免费，以及这将如何改变地球，你住在卢旺达，你距离最近的医院有两个小时的路程，这很重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's in a very, very true way.
zh: 这以一种非常真实的方式存在。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think a lot of, let's say, the negativity around AI comes from people who frankly speaking are living in a very, very good existence.
zh: 所以我认为很多，比如说，围绕 AI 的消极情绪来自那些坦率地说过着非常非常好生活的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when you live on the other edge of society, yes, and I'm not like some naive person who thinks that there's no downsides of technology, we can discuss that, but I just see there's a lot more positive.
zh: 当你生活在社会的另一边缘时，是的，我不是一个天真的人，认为技术没有缺点，我们可以讨论这一点，但我只是看到有更多的积极方面。

speaker: Unknown
en: So when you ask that question, what's the next... Forget 3 to 5 years, what's the next 20 years?
zh: 所以当你问那个问题时，接下来是什么……忘掉 3 到 5 年，接下来的 20 年是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: These things that we take for granted that are bad suddenly are not there.
zh: 我们认为理所当然的那些坏事突然就不存在了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think certain diseases, certain inaccessibility to basic services suddenly start going away.
zh: 我认为某些疾病，某些无法获得基本服务的情况突然开始消失。

speaker: Unknown
en: One last example of that is you take just the fact that you can message people basically for free.
zh: 最后一个例子是，你仅仅拥有一个事实，你可以基本上免费地给人们发信息。

speaker: Unknown
en: For people old enough, this is not the norm.
zh: 对于年纪够大的人来说，这并不是常态。

speaker: Unknown
en: We came from Pakistan.
zh: 我们来自巴基斯坦。

speaker: Unknown
en: We couldn't even communicate back to Pakistan because the long distance was so expensive and so it was handwritten letters.
zh: 我们甚至无法通信回巴基斯坦，因为长途电话太贵了，所以是手写的信件。

speaker: Unknown
en: Today, you can basically contact anybody on the planet basically for free.
zh: 今天，你可以基本上免费地联系地球上的任何人。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's obvious downsides for that, but there's lots of upsides for that, which is being in touch with people that you care about and you love basically for free.
zh: 这显然有缺点，但也有很多优点，那就是基本上免费地与你关心和爱的人保持联系。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think AI has the ability to bring this abundance to many, many more people at a near free cost.
zh: 所以我认为 AI 有能力以接近免费的成本将这种丰富带给更多的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: On the flip side of this, as you pointed out, there's a lot of anxiety about what AI is going to do to the world, to jobs, robots.
zh: 另一方面，正如你指出的，有很多关于 AI 将对世界、对工作、对机器人产生什么影响的焦虑。

speaker: Unknown
en: There are these videos coming out of China with these robots with nunchucks, like the stock market is-
zh: 中国流出了一些这些拿着双节棍的机器人的视频，就像股市——

speaker: Unknown
en: You know what?
zh: 你知道吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I feel the nunchuck union is up at arms with them.
zh: 我觉得双节棍工会正全副武装地与他们对抗。

speaker: Unknown
en: How dare they?
zh: 他们怎么敢？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: But it's scary and the market's reacting more and more to just like, "Oh, wow, these companies are maybe not going to survive long-term."
zh: 但这很可怕，市场的反应越来越像，“哦，哇，这些公司可能无法长期生存。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Again, being at the center of this and building a lot of the stuff that'll get us there, how do you envision the next couple of years playing out?
zh: 同样，身处这一切的中心，并构建大量将使我们到达那里的东西，你设想未来几年会如何展开？

speaker: Unknown
en: Are you optimistic?
zh: 你乐观吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: What keeps you optimistic?
zh: 什么让你保持乐观？

speaker: Unknown
en: Any advice to people to help them stay calm through this period?
zh: 对人们有什么建议能帮助他们在这一时期保持冷静吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: So they're two separate things, anxiety around technical shift and then the public investors reacting to specific stocks they've held.
zh: 所以这是两件独立的事情，围绕技术转变的焦虑，以及公开投资者对他们持有的特定股票的反应。

speaker: Unknown
en: We have to separate those things.
zh: 我们必须把这些事情区分开。

speaker: Unknown
en: So let's talk about them separately.
zh: 所以让我们分别谈谈它们。

speaker: Unknown
en: On the first one, the core root of fear is misunderstanding.
zh: 关于第一点，恐惧的核心根源是误解。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think if you at home are very anxious about the impact of AI in some variant on your own job, the best thing that you can do is spend time to understand it and you will quickly see the limitations.
zh: 我认为如果你在家里非常担心 AI 的某种变体对你自己的工作的影响，你能做的最好的事情就是花时间去理解它，你会很快看到局限性。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's some great videos on YouTube, which are trying to get Gemini to understand what a cup is by just holding it upside down and then they do it with ChatGPT.
zh: YouTube 上有一些很棒的视频，试图让 Gemini 通过把杯子倒过来拿来理解什么是杯子，然后他们用 ChatGPT 做同样的事。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's like if the revolution is coming, the AI overlords have to first understand the top and bottom of a cup.
zh: 所以就像如果革命即将来临，AI 领主们必须首先理解杯子的顶部和底部。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so you realize that you can see the video of nunchuck wielding humanoids, which are pre-programmed and that costs $15 million to do that video.
zh: 于是你意识到你能看到挥舞双截棍的人形机器人的视频，它们是预先编程的，制作那个视频花费了 1500 万美元。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, that is true.
zh: 是的，那是真的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not fake.
zh: 它不是假的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm not implying it's fake, but it's also not what your brain kind of fills in the gaps.
zh: 我不是暗示它是假的，但它也不是你的大脑填补空白后所想的那样。

speaker: Unknown
en: You see nunchuck robots and you just feel like, well, the gap, these are sentient beings that are at their own volition going, rather than it's a bunch of motors that have been programmed to do a certain thing.
zh: 你看到双截棍机器人，你就会觉得，好吧，空白在于，这些是有感知力的生物，它们是凭自己的意志在行动，而不是一堆被编程来做某件事的电机。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you really want to be impressed, you go to a car factory and we've been doing that for 25 years.
zh: 如果你真的想印象深刻，你可以去汽车工厂，我们已经这样做了 25 年了。

speaker: Unknown
en: We have very, very advanced robots moving extremely fast to build things.
zh: 我们有非常非常先进的机器人以极快的速度来制造东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And why we don't have anxiety about the car factory, but we have anxiety about the nunchuck robots is because the human being doesn't... Like that gap, we understand the gap of a welding robot.
zh: 为什么我们对汽车工厂没有焦虑，但对双截棍机器人有焦虑，是因为人类不……就像那个空白，我们理解焊接机器人的空白。

speaker: Unknown
en: You see, "Oh, okay, that's a robot.
zh: 你看，“哦，好吧，那是一个机器人。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's been programmed to make this weld."
zh: 它被编程来进行这次焊接。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But we don't know the technology.
zh: 但我们不了解这项技术。

speaker: Unknown
en: We as in just as an individual human being living in the world.
zh: 我们指的是作为一个生活在世界上的个人。

speaker: Unknown
en: You don't know how that robot was made to do that nunchuck thing.
zh: 你不知道那个机器人是如何被做成那个双截棍动作的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so you substitute that with anxiety and fear.
zh: 所以你用焦虑和恐惧来代替它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I would really implore you to learn more about the technology and you start seeing the edges.
zh: 所以我 really 恳求你更多地了解这项技术，你就会开始看到边界。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, does that take away from the most fundamental thing that you're getting at, the string that you're pulling at?
zh: 现在，这是否剥夺了你正在触及的最根本的事情，你正在拉的那根线？

speaker: Unknown
en: Which is like, is society going to be fundamentally harmed and is this net bad for society?
zh: 这就像，社会将受到根本性的伤害，这对社会来说净是坏事吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think in any technical shift, the emergence of WhatsApp, just as an example, there are people who are damaged by that, literally companies that go away, but also humans who are damaged by the advent of that technology.
zh: 我认为在任何技术转变中，WhatsApp 的出现，举个例子，有人因此受损，字面上是消失的公司，也有人类因这项技术的出现而受损。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think as members of society and as leaders in society, we can kind of move that funnel in whichever way technology first... Remove the word AI.
zh: 所以我认为作为社会的成员和作为社会的领导者，我们可以通过技术首先……移除 AI 这个词来向任何方向移动那个漏斗。

speaker: Unknown
en: AI is such a emotional word, because it's wrapped in these things you don't know.
zh: AI 是一个如此感性的词，因为它被包裹在你不知道的这些事情中。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so that fear then kind of deforms.
zh: 所以那种恐惧就会某种程度地变形。

speaker: Unknown
en: So let's just say technology.
zh: 所以我们就说技术吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think it's up to us to recognize this technology can be used for good and technology can be used for bad.
zh: 所以我认为我们要认识到这项技术可以用于善，技术也可以用于恶。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that's where really the focus is.
zh: 我认为这才是真正的关注点。

speaker: Unknown
en: So get to know it and then actively make the technology be used for good as a participant, whether you're a founder or all the way as an individual employee or citizen of a large company.
zh: 所以去了解它，然后积极地让技术被用于善，作为一个参与者，无论你是创始人还是一直到作为大公司的个人员工或公民。

speaker: Unknown
en: Then on the second part of the question about public investors and stuff, I don't have any particular research on this, but this is what my guess is what's actually happened.
zh: 然后关于问题中关于公开投资者等的第二部分，我对此没有任何特别的研究，但这就是我的猜测，实际发生的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Beyond being an engineer, which is my core identity, for lack of better word, I was also, I did an MBA at Harvard.
zh: 除了作为一名工程师，这是我的核心身份，恕我直言，我也在哈佛读过 MBA。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so that was the first time that this, let's say... I didn't come from very, very wealthy upbringings.
zh: 那是我第一次……我不是来自非常非常富裕的成长背景。

speaker: Unknown
en: And this was the first time when I went to Harvard, I saw that world, that world of people having private jets and stuff.
zh: 那是我第一次去哈佛时，我看到了那个世界，那个人们拥有私人飞机等的世界。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was a really eye-opening experience for me.
zh: 这对我来说是一次真正开阔眼界的经历。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the real world I was exposed to was high finance and how high finance works.
zh: 但我接触到的真实世界是高级金融以及高级金融是如何运作的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you might think, as I did from far away, that folks at hedge funds or at large public equities funds are extremely nuanced and thoughtful.
zh: 你可能会认为，就像我远远地想的那样，对冲基金或大型公共股票基金的人非常细致和深思熟虑。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they are on whiteboards with extremely deep and maybe even theoretical math to figure out, should they buy or sell Figma?
zh: 他们在白板上用极其深奥甚至可能是理论数学来计算，他们应该买入还是卖出 Figma？

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's not actually how it works.
zh: 但实际上并不是这样运作的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Really what these folks are is, and in this specific case, I think what's happening is they buy and sell stock.
zh: 这些人的真实情况是，在这个具体案例中，我认为正在发生的是他们买卖股票。

speaker: Unknown
en: They are smart people and they do work hard.
zh: 他们是聪明人，他们工作也很努力。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not to take that away, but they don't have a fundamental edge that you would assume that somebody who sits in Skyscraper in New York has.
zh: 这不是为了否定他们，但他们并没有你会假设的坐在纽约摩天大楼里的人所拥有的根本优势。

speaker: Unknown
en: And by the way, that's why retail investors have become such a active and significant part of the market.
zh: 顺便说一句，这就是为什么散户投资者已经成为市场上如此活跃和重要的一部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: So those folks have gone to AI consultants and have gone to people who are literally developers at these firms, and they'll do something like, "Hey, why don't you build me this app in a week?"
zh: 所以这些人去找了 AI 咨询师，找了那些字面上就是这些公司开发者的人，他们会做一些类似的事情，“嘿，你为什么不一周内给我构建这个应用？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And then this consultancy will come back with an app, which kind of looks like maybe a Figma or another, some web app and said, "Hedge fund manager," they're like, "well..." And then if the company was sitting there, they would say, "No, no, this just looks like my app, but this is actually not my app.
zh: 然后这个咨询公司会带回来一个应用，看起来有点像 Figma 或其他的，一些网络应用，并说，“对冲基金经理，”他们会说，“好吧……”然后如果那家公司坐在那里，他们会说，“不，不，这只是看起来像我的应用，但这实际上不是我的应用。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not as deep.
zh: 它没那么深。

speaker: Unknown
en: It doesn't have all these things.
zh: 它没有所有这些东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's the integrations with all these other systems."
zh: 这里有很多与所有其他系统的集成。"

speaker: Unknown
en: But for the public investor buy, they're like, "Yeah, but it only took a few weeks or a month to build this.
zh: 但对于公开市场买入者来说，他们会说，「是的，但这只花了几个星期或一个月就构建出来了。

speaker: Unknown
en: It took you 500 engineers a couple of years."
zh: 你们花了 500 名工程师好几年时间。」

speaker: Unknown
en: This AI thing could be real and the things I'm reading on X about just vibe coding your way to replace billion dollar companies, that might be the case and the market immediately prices in that risk."
zh: 这个 AI 东西可能是真的，而且我在 X 上读到的关于仅靠 vibe coding 就能取代十亿美元公司的那些说法，那可能是事实，市场立刻就把这种风险计入价格了。"

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's where that selloff comes from.
zh: 那就是抛售的来源。

speaker: Unknown
en: That doesn't necessarily mean all of those.
zh: 这并不一定意味着所有这些。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just within the last 24 hours, I had a... I can't say his name, but a very, let's say, calibrated investor who said, "This is the time to buy because these companies are not actually going away."
zh: 就在过去的 24 小时里，我遇到了一个……我不能说他的名字，但一位非常，可以说，审慎的投资者，他说，「现在是买入的时候了，因为这些公司实际上并不会消失。」

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think those are two... Anxiety within society and the selloff are two very different things.
zh: 所以我认为这两个是……社会的焦虑和抛售是两件非常不同的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're motivated by different things.
zh: 它们的动机不同。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're part of the larger AI narrative, but I wouldn't conflate those two things.
zh: 它们是更大 AI 叙事的一部分，但我不会把这两件事混为一谈。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not that the hedge fund investor is like, "I'm worried about society, sell service now."
zh: 并不是说对冲基金投资者会想，「我担心社会，现在卖出服务。」

speaker: Unknown
en: It's different than that, or at least that's my impression.
zh: 并不是那样，或者至少那是我的印象。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is the alpha we've been talking about, time to buy.
zh: 这就是我们一直在谈论的 alpha，是时候买入了。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is not investment advice.
zh: 这不是投资建议。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, that's really good advice.
zh: 那真是很好的建议。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the real advice is to fight fear.
zh: 我认为真正的建议是与恐惧作斗争。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I feel that anxiety, especially when I go to Michigan and outside of people in the Silicon Valley bubble, it's like just try to learn a little bit about the technology that you're afraid of and you'll start seeing some of the edges.
zh: 我能感受到那种焦虑，特别是当我去密歇根州以及在硅谷泡沫之外的人群中时，就像是试着稍微了解一下你害怕的技术，你就会开始看到其中的一些边缘。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love your point about how self-driving cars are essentially robots.
zh: 我喜欢你关于自动驾驶汽车本质上是智能体的观点。

speaker: Unknown
en: We don't call them that, but they're robots.
zh: 我们不这样叫它们，但它们就是智能体。

speaker: Unknown
en: Absolutely.
zh: 绝对的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you see a nunchuck-wielding robot, a self-driving car doing bad things could be very dangerous already.
zh: 你看到一个挥舞双节棍的智能体，一辆做坏事的自动驾驶汽车已经可能非常危险。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so that's a really good reframe that if you just think of it's just another robot and it's been really good for us.
zh: 所以这是一个非常好的重构，如果你只把它看作只是另一种智能体，并且它对我们一直非常好。

speaker: Unknown
en: And by the way, the self-driving thing, as an example, whichever way you slice the statistics that are available from self-driving companies, they're supremely, supremely more safe than human drivers.
zh: 顺便说一下，自动驾驶这件事，作为一个例子，无论你如何看待自动驾驶公司提供的统计数据，它们都比人类司机安全得多，得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I do believe in 20 or 30 years, not that much longer, we'll look back and we'll kind of be like, it's kind of like we think about child labor.
zh: 我确实相信在 20 年或 30 年后，不用太久，我们会回过头来，我们会有点像，这有点像我们看待童工一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: In a post-Industrial Revolution, that was a normal thing.
zh: 在工业革命后，那是件正常的事。

speaker: Unknown
en: You would send kids who are in middle school to go work.
zh: 你会送上初中的孩子去工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: It happens in third world countries today.
zh: 这在今天的第三世界国家也会发生。

speaker: Unknown
en: There isn't a lot of emotion behind it.
zh: 这背后没有太多情绪。

speaker: Unknown
en: It is not considered to be exploitative because you have no choice.
zh: 它不被认为是剥削性的，因为你别无选择。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think we'll look back in 25, 30 years, we're like, people were just tired, under the influence, extremely stressed, going through a traumatic life experience, and then they jump into a car like that.
zh: 我认为我们会在 25 年、30 年后回顾，我们会说，人们只是疲惫、受到酒精影响、压力极大，经历着创伤性生活经历，然后他们跳进那样的车里。

speaker: Unknown
en: It is crazy.
zh: 这太疯狂了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And just for everyone, everyone should really emotionally think about it.
zh: 而且为了每个人，每个人都应该真正地在情感上思考一下。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just in the United States, over 30,000 people will die in the next year from these accidents.
zh: 就在美国，明年将有超过 30,000 人死于这些事故。

speaker: Unknown
en: The old Stalin line, it's like one death is a tragedy, a million is a statistic, and we just let the statistic kind of go over our head like, "Oh, it's 30,000 people."
zh: 那句斯大林的老话，就像一个人的死亡是悲剧，一百万人的死亡是统计数字，我们只是让那个统计数字从我们头脑中溜走，就像，「哦，那是 30,000 人。」

speaker: Unknown
en: But if you ever have talked to a family of somebody who went through a tragedy like a car accident, it's unbelievable.
zh: 但如果你曾经与经历过车祸悲剧的人的家庭交谈过，那是难以置信的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And suddenly, all the fear of AI robots goes away and you really see that human impact and you realize actually us driving doesn't make sense.
zh: 突然间，所有对 AI 智能体的恐惧都消失了，你真正看到了那种人类影响，并且你意识到实际上我们驾驶并不合理。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's not for any other reason than literally people die.
zh: 除了字面上人们会死之外，没有其他原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've become a huge... I have a Tesla and I've just used self-driving all the time now.
zh: 我已经成了一个巨大的……我有一辆特斯拉，而且我现在一直都在使用自动驾驶。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just like a few months ago it got very good and it used to be nerve-wracking and now it's like, "Wow, this is much better than I am."
zh: 就在几个月前它变得非常好，以前它很伤脑筋，现在就像是，「哇，这比我强多了。」

speaker: Unknown
en: And you're not doing driving as a job.
zh: 而且你不是把驾驶作为一份工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: Imagine if you're a commercial truck driver or you work in a mine or you work... There, a little bit of intelligence, a helping hand in that very dangerous task, it's incredible.
zh: 想象一下，如果你是一名商业卡车司机，或者你在矿山工作，或者你在……那里，有一点智能，在那个非常危险的任务中帮把手，这是不可思议的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think there's something about the human brain where when you bring up that reality of self-driving trucks, immediately people are like, "Well, what about the trucking jobs?"
zh: 我认为人脑有一些特性，当你提出自动驾驶卡车的现实时，人们立刻会说，「但是，卡车运输的工作怎么办？」

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, needless to say, we don't have enough people who want to do that job.
zh: 现在，不用说，我们没有足够的人想做那份工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: So leave that fact to the side.
zh: 所以把那个事实放在一边。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the fact that you really focus on is the fact that people die from trucking accidents.
zh: 我认为你真正关注的事实是人们死于卡车事故。

speaker: Unknown
en: We can't throw out the baby with the bathwater.
zh: 我们不能因噎废食。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think I implore everybody who thinks about AI broadly and physical AI specifically to always recognize that your monkey brain is programmed because of hundreds of thousands of years of living out in the wild and being in the cave that when you hear the rustle in the bush, that is you think it's a snake because that's what our ancestors were programmed.
zh: 所以我想恳请所有广泛思考 AI 特别是物理 AI 的人，始终要认识到你的猴子大脑之所以被编程，是因为我们在野外生活了数十万年，住在山洞里，当你在灌木丛中听到沙沙声时，你认为那是条蛇，因为这就是我们的祖先被编程的结果。

speaker: Unknown
en: So now when something new enters our psyche, your view isn't, well, if mines became autonomous, well, wouldn't that lose jobs?
zh: 所以现在，当新事物进入我们的心灵时，你的观点并不是，嗯，如果矿山变得自主，那不会失去工作吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, those are awful jobs that people die in.
zh: 这就像，那些是人们会在其中死去的糟糕工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the best evidence is that people don't want to work in them.
zh: 最好的证据是人们不想在这些地方工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's the best evidence.
zh: 那是最好的证据。

speaker: Unknown
en: Nobody's clamoring to go work in a mine in a remote area.
zh: 没有人争相去偏远地区的矿井工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so intelligence can help make that reality much, much better.
zh: 所以智能体可以帮助让那个现实变得好得多，好得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: People are seeing AI advance in all these different ways on the software side and they see all these models being released.
zh: 人们在软件端看到 AI 以所有这些不同的方式进步，他们看到所有这些模型被发布。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's driving 100% of people's code now.
zh: 它现在驱动着 100% 的人的代码。

speaker: Unknown
en: What's really cool about you is you see the hardware side of this, and I think one of the biggest changes to our lives will probably be robots walking around doing things for us.
zh: 你真正很酷的地方在于你看到了这一点的硬件端，我认为对我们生活的最大改变之一可能就是机器人四处走动为我们做事。

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you have a sense of just how close we are just robots around us day-to-day?
zh: 你有感觉我们离日常生活中身边就有机器人有多近吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: So I would think about this, the framing here matters again on a spectrum.
zh: 所以我会思考这个问题，这里的框架在一个频谱上再次很重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there are robots around us like Roombas, that clean your carpet where you're sleeping.
zh: 所以我们身边有像 Roomba 这样的机器人，在你睡觉时清洁你的地毯。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's robots around you when you make a coffee, that's an automated machine that is taking an input and doing a bunch of things based on what you need.
zh: 当你煮咖啡时你身边有机器人，那是一台自动化的机器，接收输入并根据你的需要做一堆事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: So what you're really talking about is how fast can we go up that spectrum to where you have a robot that can take on lots of tasks with little guidance.
zh: 所以你真正谈论的是我们能在那个频谱上多快地前进到你拥有一个可以在很少指导下承担大量任务的机器人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the way that I would think about this is, let's say we're sitting in, this podcast is happening not in 2026, but 2006, and you're asking me the same question about mobile and you say, "Well, mobile is coming."
zh: 我思考这个问题的方式是，假设我们坐在，这个播客不是发生在 2026 年，而是 2006 年，你问我关于移动端的同样问题，你说，「嗯，移动端来了。」

speaker: Unknown
en: This is, remember, pre-iPhone, which comes out in 07, everyone has got those flip phones.
zh: 这是，记得，前 iPhone 时代，它出来是在 07 年，大家都有那些翻盖手机。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we have mobile.
zh: 所以我们有了移动端。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not like a completely... So we have some robots around us already, but it's like, okay, so when are we going to... And you asked me in 2006, "When are we going to get that Star Trek phone that can do everything?"
zh: 它不像一个完全……所以我们身边已经有一些机器人了，但就像，好吧，那么我们什么时候要……你在 2006 年问我，「我们什么时候能得到那个能做所有事的《星际迷航》手机？」

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think at that time, I would say, because I don't even know the iPhone is coming a year later, I would say, "Well, Lenny, I don't know.
zh: 我想在那个时候，我会说，因为我甚至不知道 iPhone 一年后会来，我会说，「嗯，Lenny，我不知道。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe it's one to five years."
zh: 也许是一到五年。」

speaker: Unknown
en: And if it's not five years later that Uber, WhatsApp, Instagram, Snapchat are all products and they're being consumed by many, many, many millions of people.
zh: 如果不是五年后，Uber、WhatsApp、Instagram、Snapchat 都是产品，并且被许许多多、许多人消费。

speaker: Unknown
en: So what happens when you think about sitting in 2006 and why can't your brain figure out that Instagram is coming?
zh: 所以当你思考坐在 2006 年时会发生什么，为什么你的大脑无法弄清楚 Instagram 要来了？

speaker: Unknown
en: Instagram is very hard to even conceive without phones that have an app store, have cameras on both sides are available, generally available that lots of people have it.
zh: 没有有应用商店、两边都有摄像头、普遍可用的、许多人拥有的手机，Instagram 很难甚至去构想。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the fact that people are comfortable being on social networks.
zh: 而且人们对社交网络感到舒适这一事实。

speaker: Unknown
en: In 2006, it's still an early thing.
zh: 在 2006 年，这仍然是一件早期的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is pre-Twitter and Facebook is not that big and MySpace is, but it's not the same type of private kind of communication.
zh: 这是前 Twitter 时代，Facebook 还没那么大，MySpace 是很大，但它不是那种同一类型的私密的沟通。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so the point I'm making is I think it can come pretty fast, but the way and the form factor will come is hard to pick just like it's hard to figure out Instagram's going to happen because the intelligence in that particular type of hardware, which will be generally available, that's a keyword, generally available, is really going to impact the use cases.
zh: 所以我要说的点是，我认为它可能来得相当快，但它来的方式和形式将很难预测，就像很难弄清楚 Instagram 会发生一样，因为那种特定类型硬件中的智能，它将是普遍可用的，那是一个关键词，普遍可用的，真的会影响到用例。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think the most obvious use cases that will come early are going to be use cases where you get the most amount of bang for buck.
zh: 所以我认为会最早到来的最明显的用例，将会是你获得最高性价比的用例。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the bang for buck is a car that drives itself or a mining robot, which is a mining vehicle, which is now intelligent.
zh: 而且性价比是一辆自动驾驶的汽车或者一个采矿机器人，它是一个采矿车辆，它现在是智能的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the reason is all that, let's say engineering required to make this giant machine that moves dirt has already been done.
zh: 原因是所有，假设让这个移动泥土的巨型机器工作所需的工程已经完成了。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's been done over the last 50, 60 years.
zh: 它在过去 50、60 年里已经完成了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So then you're just putting a little bit of intelligence into it and leveraging everything else that the companies and people have developed.
zh: 所以你只是把一点点智能放入其中，并利用公司和人们已经开发的所有其他东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think, and I'm not just pitching my own book.
zh: 所以我认为，而且我不只是推销我自己的书。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, we're a physical AI company.
zh: 我的意思是，我们是一家物理 AI 公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: I continue to believe that I think our brain emotionally loves the humanoid concept because we're monkeys.
zh: 我继续相信我们的大脑在情感上喜爱人形概念，因为我们是猴子。

speaker: Unknown
en: But actually, just more pragmatically, it's actually just putting intelligence into things that already exist all around us.
zh: 但实际上，只是更实用主义一点，实际上只是把智能放入我们已经存在于我们周围的所有事物中。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then once that happens, then new applications will emerge, which I think we'll talk about in five to seven years, which we'll start seeing.
zh: 然后一旦那发生了，新的应用将涌现，我认为我们将在五到七年后谈论它们，我们将开始看到它们。

speaker: Unknown
en: So let's just move forward five to seven years and let's see what reality exists.
zh: 所以让我们直接向前快进五到七年，让我们看看存在什么现实。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then maybe we can try to jump into the future from there.
zh: 然后也许我们可以尝试从那里跳向未来。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think generally speaking, every single car company on the planet right now is working on a product that's like a Tesla FSD product, every single car company there without exception.
zh: 我认为一般来说，地球上现在每一家汽车公司都在开发一个像特斯拉 FSD 产品那样的产品，那里的每一家汽车公司无一例外。

speaker: Unknown
en: Many, many companies are working in versions of that that become fully autonomous within a cheap sensor suite.
zh: 许许多多的公司正在开发它的版本，这些版本在廉价的传感器套件内变成完全自主的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the fundamental difference, just to simplify it all, the Tesla approach versus the Waymo approach, just to really keep it simple, is the Waymo approach is lots of sensors and lots of compute and maps.
zh: 所以根本的区别，只是为了简化一切，特斯拉的方法与 Waymo 的方法，为了真正保持简单，就是 Waymo 的方法是大量的传感器和大量的计算和地图。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the Tesla version is very few sensors, no maps, no high fidelity maps and just generalizing here and cheaper compete for the lack of better word.
zh: 而特斯拉的版本是非常少的传感器，没有地图，没有高保真地图，这里只是概括，用廉价来竞争，为了缺乏更好的词。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the Tesla version of a product, this is in the industry is called an L2++ product, is going to be available everywhere because it's literally cheaper and it doesn't require HD maps.
zh: 而且特斯拉版本的产品，这是在行业里被称为 L2++ 产品，将随处可用，因为它真的更便宜，而且它不需要 HD 地图。

speaker: Unknown
en: The Waymo product functions better in a geographically constrained area.
zh: Waymo 的产品在地理受限的区域功能更好。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you fast forward five years, both of these types of technologies will be much more ubiquitous.
zh: 所以你快进五年，这两种类型的技术都将更加无处不在。

speaker: Unknown
en: L2++ and L4 will be much more ubiquitous, not only in the Bay Area or in parts of China, but really globally.
zh: L2++ 和 L4 将更加无处不在，不仅在海湾地区或在中国部分地区，而是真正在全球。

speaker: Unknown
en: There are companies working on this globally.
zh: 有公司在全球范围内正在做这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: So now, I don't know if you remember, but NAV systems used to be a big deal in cars.
zh: 所以现在，我不知道你是否记得，但 NAV 系统曾经在汽车里是一件大事。

speaker: Unknown
en: You would pay thousands of dollars and NAV systems were kind of the thing that everybody wanted.
zh: 你会花费数千美元，而且 NAV 系统是每个人都想要的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're at that moment for L2++ systems where people are willing to pay thousands of dollars for a semi-automated vehicle.
zh: 我们对于 L2++ 系统就处于那个时刻，人们愿意为一辆半自动化的车辆花费数千美元。

speaker: Unknown
en: It will not be a long time.
zh: 这不会是很长的时间。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're already seeing this happen in China, where the downward pricing pressure for that autonomous product, for the lack of better word, will become close to free.
zh: 你已经看到这在中国发生了，这种自主产品的向下定价压力，因为缺乏更好的词，将变得接近免费。

speaker: Unknown
en: So now you fast-forward five to seven years and every car has some level of autonomy.
zh: 所以现在你快进五到七年，每辆车都有某种程度的自主性。

speaker: Unknown
en: So now you have to mentally live in that reality that everybody who's buying a car, they just get FSD with it.
zh: 所以现在你必须在那个现实中生活，即每个买车的人，他们只是顺带得到 FSD。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now you start seeing a different world because now the average person isn't wondering is self-driving in a car.
zh: 现在你开始看到一个不同的世界，因为现在普通人不再怀疑车里的自动驾驶。

speaker: Unknown
en: They use it all the time.
zh: 他们一直在使用它。

speaker: Unknown
en: They don't wonder, are NAVs... And so what you have in NAV systems is a CarPlay emerges and Android Auto emerges, and it's very natural.
zh: 他们不怀疑，是否 NAVs……所以你在 NAV 系统中看到的是 CarPlay 出现了，Android Auto 出现了，这很自然。

speaker: Unknown
en: People are like, "Oh, I have my phone.
zh: 人们就像，“噢，我有我的手机。

speaker: Unknown
en: I just plug it in."
zh: 我只是把它插进去。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And it wasn't a big revolution, but the CarPlay and Android Auto revolution is actually huge.
zh: 这不是一场巨大的革命，但 CarPlay 和 Android Auto 革命实际上是巨大的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It brings free navigation and free applications to your car and it's fairly ubiquitous.
zh: 它将免费的导航和免费的应用程序带到你的车里，而且它相当普遍。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think the next thing that happens in five to seven years is then full autonomy becomes the thing that everyone expects.
zh: 所以我认为接下来的五到七年发生的事情是，完全自主性将成为每个人都期待的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think all of that, and you will see a clear decrease in injuries and death because of that, because you have some intelligence helping you, help you drive.
zh: 所以我认为所有这些，你将看到受伤和死亡的明显减少，因为那样你有一些智能帮助你，帮助你驾驶。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, again, I'm using the consumer of vehicle analogies just so people can understand it, but this is the same in construction.
zh: 现在，再次，我使用车辆的消费者类比只是为了人们能理解它，但这在建筑业中是一样的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's the same in mining.
zh: 在采矿业是一样的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's the same in defense.
zh: 在国防领域是一样的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's in every one of these verticals.
zh: 在每一个这些垂直领域中都是如此。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's these big physical machines that humans are interacting with.
zh: 有这些大型物理机器，人类正在与之交互。

speaker: Unknown
en: That teaming up with that machine is the future.
zh: 与那台机器的合作就是未来。

speaker: Unknown
en: The productivity unlock from just you looking at a machine, not like a sentient being, but almost like a physical agent of something you're trying to accomplish, unlocks things that I think are very hard to think about.
zh: 生产力的释放来自于你看着一台机器，不像是一个有感知能力的存在，但几乎像一个物理智能体，用于你试图完成的事情，解锁了我认为很难想象的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I love things like Moltbook and I love, let's say, OpenClaw revolution that's happening for the lack of a better word, but I think the big impact, that's still such a small part of society.
zh: 所以我喜欢像 Moltbook 这样的东西，也喜欢，比如说，OpenClaw 革命正在发生，因为缺乏更好的词，但我认为大的影响，这仍然是社会的一小部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: My barometer of impact is like you go to the Detroit airport and you sit in a gate and you look around and you're like, how many people here are using OpenClaw?
zh: 我的影响力衡量标准就像你去底特律机场，你坐在一个登机口，你环顾四周，你会想，这里有多少人在使用 OpenClaw？

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's like, let me look into like, you might be the only person who knows what that is.
zh: 就像，让我看看，你可能是唯一知道那是什么的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And whereas everybody, they're living their lives there.
zh: 而其他人，他们在那里过着自己的生活。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so it's like to them, actually the impact of AI is going to be in this physical world.
zh: 所以就像对他们来说，实际上 AI 的影响将在这个物理世界中。

speaker: Unknown
en: I see you also have a... Yeah, there you go.
zh: 我看到你也有一个……是的，给你。

speaker: Unknown
en: Check it out.
zh: 看看吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm a convert, OpenClaw.
zh: 我是一个皈依者，OpenClaw。

speaker: Unknown
en: There you go.
zh: 给你。

speaker: Unknown
en: Perfect.
zh: 完美。

speaker: Unknown
en: Perfect.
zh: 完美。

speaker: Unknown
en: Peter's coming on the pod soon, so I got some [inaudible 00:28:07].
zh: Peter 很快就要上播客了，所以我有一些 [听不清 00:28:07]。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think the real impact of AI in the next 5 to 10 years really is going to be in farming, in mining, in construction and self-driving trucks.
zh: 所以我认为 AI 在未来 5 到 10 年的真正影响将真正出现在农业、采矿、建筑和自动驾驶卡车上。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's where you're going to have a real impact.
zh: 那就是你将产生真正影响的地方。

speaker: Unknown
en: Though I think... I mean, I love this stuff that's happening on these platforms, but it's still segregated to frankly developers and a very, very small part of society.
zh: 虽然我认为……我是说，我喜欢这些平台上发生的事情，但它仍然实际上是隔离给开发者和社会的一小部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: I wasn't planning to spend so much time here, but this is extremely interesting.
zh: 我不打算在这里花这么多时间，但这非常有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think it's important for people to hear from folks like you about where things are heading, because as I said, everyone's just like, "What is happening?
zh: 我认为人们听听像你这样的人关于事情走向的看法是很重要的，因为正如我所说，每个人都只是像，“正在发生什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What is going to be my future?"
zh: 我的未来会是什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: The jobs piece is really interesting.
zh: 工作这事儿真的很有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: And a thread that has emerged on this podcast recently is that people are afraid AI will take their jobs, but in reality, AI is coming just in time to save us because populations are declining, people are aging, and we need something to help us there.
zh: 这个播客上最近出现的一个线索是，人们害怕 AI 会抢走他们的工作，但实际上，AI 的到来正是时候来拯救我们，因为人口在下降，人们在变老，我们需要东西来帮助我们。

speaker: Unknown
en: I know this is something you... And this is something Marc talked about and you're really close with him.
zh: 我知道这是你……这是 Marc 谈论过的，你和他关系很密切。

speaker: Unknown
en: Help us feel better about just how AI isn't going to take our jobs and actually going to save us.
zh: 帮我们感觉好一点，关于 AI 不会抢走我们的工作，实际上将拯救我们。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think honestly speaking, these industries, they need autonomy and it couldn't come soon enough, frankly speaking.
zh: 我认为老实说，这些行业，它们需要自主性，而且坦率地说，这来得越快越好。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is not like people are not fighting for those trucking jobs.
zh: 这不是像人们不在争夺那些卡车运输工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you look at farmers, the average age of a farmer is in their late 50s, 58 or so.
zh: 如果你看看农民，农民的平均年龄在 50 多岁，58 岁左右。

speaker: Unknown
en: What does that mean in 10 years from now?
zh: 那意味着从现在起 10 年会怎样？

speaker: Unknown
en: That means many of those farmers are going to be retiring if they're not already retired, and 20 years we have even a bigger problem.
zh: 那意味着那些农民中的许多人将退休，如果他们还没退休的话，而在 20 年我们有一个更大的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: That, by the way, is every verticals like that.
zh: 顺便说一句，每个垂直领域都是那样的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And my hypothesis here, sometimes people say McDonald's can't hire or the mine and local quarry can't hire, and where are all the people?
zh: 我在这里的假设，有时人们说麦当劳无法雇佣，或者矿山和当地的采石场无法雇佣，人都去哪了？

speaker: Unknown
en: The people are still here.
zh: 人还在那里。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think just the trade off is just not worth it anymore.
zh: 我认为只是这种权衡不再值得了。

speaker: Unknown
en: In the 1980s and the 1990s, doing the long haul trucking job was what the family has to sacrifice, the father not being there for days and weeks on end.
zh: 在 20 世纪 80 年代和 90 年代，从事长途卡车运输工作意味着家庭必须做出牺牲，父亲连续几天、几周不在家。

speaker: Unknown
en: And today, that same working class family can make that decision and say, "You know what?
zh: 而今天，同样的工薪阶层家庭可以做出决定并说，"你知道吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I will drive for Uber or DoorDash and I'm willing to do that because I can turn that app off and pick up my kid and I prioritize that."
zh: 我会为 Uber 或 DoorDash 开车，我愿意这样做，因为我可以关闭那个应用程序去接我的孩子，我优先考虑那个。"

speaker: Unknown
en: That is where I think this kind of intelligence kind of revolution in the real world is really, I think, is going to fill those gaps in rather than an entire industry is suddenly gone and it's just automated.
zh: 我认为这就是现实世界中的这种智能革命真正的意义所在，我认为它将填补这些空白，而不是整个行业突然消失并完全自动化。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't believe that future, mainly because the realities of actually replacing an entire industry's robots is still, that's too complex.
zh: 我不相信那个未来，主要是因为用机器人实际替代整个行业的现实仍然太复杂了。

speaker: Unknown
en: One day it will happen, but it's not happening anytime soon.
zh: 总有一天会发生，但不会很快发生。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the entire society will be different by that point.
zh: 但到那时整个社会都将不同。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think, again, use the Industrial Revolution as a good version of that.
zh: 我认为，再次以工业革命作为这方面的一个很好的版本。

speaker: Unknown
en: The earlier question, if I'm somebody who is not in the AI ecosystem and I have this anxiety, and how would I deal with it?
zh: 回到之前的问题，如果我是 AI 生态系统之外的人，我有这种焦虑，我将如何应对它？

speaker: Unknown
en: Reading history books is a great way to really understand how society deals with this.
zh: 阅读历史书是真正理解社会如何应对这一点的绝佳方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's a lot of literature because industrial revolution doesn't happen like in the dawn of Christianity where not many people are writing and not many people are reading.
zh: 有很多文学作品，因为工业革命并不是像在基督教黎明时期那样，没有多少人在写作，也没有多少人在阅读。

speaker: Unknown
en: Lots of people are writing, lots of people are reading in the last 150 years.
zh: 在过去 150 年里，很多人在写作，很多人在阅读。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you can read both the people who are impacted by the Industrial Revolution, people who are benefiting from it.
zh: 你既可以阅读受到工业革命影响的人，也可以阅读从中受益的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And writ large, it's a very positive experience.
zh: 总的来说，这是一次非常积极的体验。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that doesn't mean... Again, there are downsides.
zh: 这并不意味着……再次强调，也有负面影响。

speaker: Unknown
en: We should mitigate the downsides.
zh: 我们应该减轻负面影响。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the thing that we can't do, and this is maybe specifically as America or society, as the global population as a whole, there's this impetus to say, "We got to pump this break on..." Again, don't say AI, say technology, pump this break on technology.
zh: 但我们不能做的事情是，这可能是作为美国或社会，作为全球人口整体，有一种冲动说，"我们必须踩下刹车……" 再次强调，不要说 AI，说技术，踩下技术的刹车。

speaker: Unknown
en: The issue then is the American economy really ends up stuttering, and that impacts the lowest end of the labor market way more than anybody else.
zh: 那样的问题在于美国经济最终会陷入停滞，这对劳动力市场的最低端影响最大，远超其他任何人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so in the attempt to help the people who are the most marginalized, we actually hurt them the most.
zh: 因此，为了帮助我们试图帮助的最边缘化的人群，我们实际上最严重地伤害了他们。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the statistics between Europe and America are pretty explicit.
zh: 欧洲和美国之间的统计数据非常明确。

speaker: Unknown
en: But in the last decade, basically the American economy is now grown at a much higher pace.
zh: 但在过去十年中，基本上美国经济现在的增长速度快得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that growth hasn't come from Detroit, Michigan.
zh: 这种增长并非来自密歇根州的底特律。

speaker: Unknown
en: That growth has come from Mountain View and it's come from Sunnyvale.
zh: 这种增长来自山景城，也来自桑尼维尔。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's come from the Bay Area, which is another way of saying it's because of new frontier technologies.
zh: 它来自湾区，这是另一种说法，即是因为新的前沿技术。

speaker: Unknown
en: So putting brakes on frontier technologies because we're afraid of unintended consequences will actually have real intended consequences on people who we're trying to help the most.
zh: 因此，仅仅因为害怕意外后果而对前沿技术踩下刹车，实际上会对我们试图提供最多帮助的人产生真正的、预期的后果。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the reality is very, very fundamental, in a future that does not take care of the average worker and the average person in America will have much bigger problems.
zh: 现实是非常、非常根本的，在一个不照顾普通工人的未来，美国的普通人将有更大的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we need a solution that takes that into account, but that solution isn't just pump the brakes, AI is bad, or frontier technology is bad, or technology's bad, or whatever thing that you don't like.
zh: 所以我们需要一个考虑到这一点的解决方案，但那个解决方案不仅仅是踩刹车，AI 是坏的，或者前沿技术是坏的，或者技术是坏的，或者任何你不喜欢的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that'll have really, really bad consequences.
zh: 我认为那将产生非常、非常糟糕的后果。

speaker: Unknown
en: One of the reasons that we don't pump the brakes is just fear of China and competition with China, the nunchuck robots being a recent example of like, "Oh shit."
zh: 我们不踩刹车的原因之一仅仅是害怕中国以及与中国的竞争，"双截棍机器人"（nunchuck robots）就是最近的一个例子，就像，"噢，该死。"

speaker: Unknown
en: I know you have a contrarian take on just how much of a threat China is and how they're approaching things.
zh: 我知道你对中国的威胁有多大以及他们如何处理事情有一个相反的观点。

speaker: Unknown
en: The summary version of this is I think the way we recently read as a company, we read this book, House of Huawei, which is just a really great interesting book.
zh: 这一点的总结版本是，我认为我们要最近作为公司阅读了《华为数据中心》（House of Huawei）这本书，这是一本非常有趣的书。

speaker: Unknown
en: And Huawei is a really amazing company for the reason that it makes great technology.
zh: 华为是一家非常了不起的公司，因为它制造很棒的技术。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the couple hundred thousand people that work at Huawei, about a quarter of them are members of the Communist Party.
zh: 但在华为工作的几十万人中，大约有四分之一是共产党员。

speaker: Unknown
en: And Huawei's goal is not to grow profits or shareholders.
zh: 华为的目标不是增加利润或造福股东。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a private company.
zh: 它是一家私人公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's really an extension of the state.
zh: 它实际上是国家的一种延伸。

speaker: Unknown
en: So literally the name Huawei means China's ambition.
zh: 所以从字面上看，华为这个名字意味着中国的雄心。

speaker: Unknown
en: So imagine if you had a company called MAGA and half of the company or a quarter of the company was a certain political party and they said, "Our goal isn't to make profits or to... Our goal is just the expansion of..." It's not even a company anymore.
zh: 想象一下，如果你有一家叫 MAGA 的公司，公司的一半或四分之一是某个政党，他们说，"我们的目标不是盈利或……我们的目标只是扩张……" 它甚至不再是一家公司了。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's something else.
zh: 它是别的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think we incorrectly, when we specifically speak of Americans, we think about China, we impart our understanding of markets and companies onto China.
zh: 所以我认为当我们特别是谈论美国人时，我们错误地看待中国，我们将对市场和公司的理解投射到中国身上。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we think Huawei, since they make phones, they must be just like Apple.
zh: 所以我们认为华为既然制造手机，他们一定就像苹果一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, no, no, no, no.
zh: 就像，不，不，不，不。

speaker: Unknown
en: Actually, that's not like Apple at all.
zh: 实际上，那一点也不像苹果。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think the first thing I would implore everybody who thinks about China, especially with anxiety in America is you're not comparing companies to companies.
zh: 所以我认为我想恳求每一个思考中国的人，尤其是带着焦虑的美国人，要注意你正在拿公司与公司进行比较。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is not apples to apples.
zh: 这不是同类比较。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is very, very different.
zh: 这非常、非常不同。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so imagine instead of thinking OpenAI is competing against DeepSeek, you say OpenAIs competing against the Chinese government.
zh: 所以想象一下，不要认为是 OpenAI 在与 DeepSeek 竞争，你要说 OpenAI 是与中国政府在竞争。

speaker: Unknown
en: Instead of Apple competing against Huawei, Apple's competing against the Chinese government.
zh: 与其说是苹果在与华为竞争，不如说是苹果在与中国政府竞争。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you can even remove the word Chinese government.
zh: 你甚至可以把“中国政府”这个词去掉。

speaker: Unknown
en: Government is the best word to define what this organization is, but it's not a for-profit, privately-owned, independent group of people who are working on projects together to build products to market.
zh: 政府是定义这个组织是什么的最佳词汇，但它不是一个为了营利、私有、独立的一群人一起致力于项目来制造产品推向市场的团体。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's the first very important thing.
zh: 所以那是第一个非常重要的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: You cannot treat China like another America, or another Europe, or another whatever.
zh: 你不能把中国当成另一个美国，或者另一个欧洲，或者其他的什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: Number two is if your goal isn't to make profits, you can do incredible research and it can be extremely compelling.
zh: 第二点是，如果你的目标不是营利，你可以做令人难以置信的研究，而且它可能非常有吸引力。

speaker: Unknown
en: But like we've seen, if the system is not sustainable, that's also not a company and that's not sustainable.
zh: 但是正如我们所见，如果这个系统是不可持续的，那也不是一家公司，那也是不可持续的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Let me give a very stark example of that.
zh: 让我给出一个非常鲜明的例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: Chinese EVs are really lauded as being this exceptionally interesting product and you constantly get the streamer of, I would say, fairly shallow analysis which says, look how good China, isn't like how bad Munich, Detroit, Tokyo, Seoul, or the other epicenters for automotive globally.
zh: 中国的电动汽车真的被赞誉为这种特别有趣的产品，并且你不断地得到，我想说，相当肤浅的分析的流，这些分析说，看看中国多好，不像慕尼黑、底特律、东京、首尔或全球其他汽车中心有多差。

speaker: Unknown
en: There is a Chinese EV-like company in America.
zh: 在美国有一家像中国电动汽车一样的公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's called Rivian, makes great products, but they lose a lot of money making those products.
zh: 它叫 Rivian，制造很棒的产品，但他们制造那些产品亏损了很多钱。

speaker: Unknown
en: And therefore, the company is not very highly valued.
zh: 因此，该公司的估值不是很高。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think if you said top 50 or top 100 companies in the Bay Area, I'm not sure Rivian would even make that list.
zh: 我想如果你说湾区前 50 或前 100 名的公司，我不确定 Rivian 甚至能进那个名单。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's not that the products are bad or the people at Rivian are incompetent or they're not working hard.
zh: 这并不是说产品不好，或者 Rivian 的人无能，或者他们工作不努力。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's just the business is a tough business.
zh: 只是这门生意是一门艰难的生意。

speaker: Unknown
en: The EV business in automotive is a tough business.
zh: 汽车领域的电动汽车生意是一门艰难的生意。

speaker: Unknown
en: So how can we hold these realities?
zh: 那么我们如何接受这些现实呢？

speaker: Unknown
en: So we say, look how amazing these Chinese EV companies are and look how bad the home team is.
zh: 所以我们说，看看这些中国电动汽车公司多么令人惊叹，再看看主队有多差。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's just because the home team is being assessed for being a business.
zh: 这只是因为主队是作为一家企业被评估的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It has to make profits.
zh: 它必须营利。

speaker: Unknown
en: And because it doesn't, it gets hammered by public investors.
zh: 因为它不营利，它受到了公开投资者的打击。

speaker: Unknown
en: The other thing is not even a company.
zh: 另一方面甚至不是一家公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, if we do it apples to apples, America just has to build great EVs.
zh: 现在，如果我们做一个公平的比较，美国只需要制造很棒的电动汽车。

speaker: Unknown
en: That means Tesla and everybody else combined and we don't care about profits, I think America would field some very good products and there would be wow products.
zh: 这意味着特斯拉和其他所有人结合在一起，我们不关心营利，我认为美国会拿出一些非常好的产品，而且会有令人惊叹的产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the comparisons are really, really off and I think that creates a misunderstanding.
zh: 所以这些比较真的、真的是偏颇的，我认为这造成了一种误解。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think then maybe the most philosophical question, can China succeed and does that mean America has to fail or vice versa?
zh: 我想那么也许是最大的哲学问题，中国能成功吗？这是否意味着美国必须失败，反之亦然？

speaker: Unknown
en: If you believe in open and free markets, you believe everybody can succeed in those markets.
zh: 如果你相信开放和自由的市场，你相信每个人都可以在那些市场中成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's been proven for over 100 years.
zh: 这一点已经被证明了超过 100 年。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think what we're experiencing right now is how does China play in that ecosystem?
zh: 我想我们现在正在经历的是中国如何在这个生态系统中发挥作用？

speaker: Unknown
en: Because I said open and free markets and those are not open and free markets.
zh: 因为我说了开放和自由的市场，而这些不是开放和自由的市场。

speaker: Unknown
en: But that doesn't necessarily mean that you have to have an antagonistic relationship.
zh: 但这并不一定意味着你们必须要有一种对抗性的关系。

speaker: Unknown
en: It certainly doesn't mean that China's incompetent, and it certainly doesn't mean that it doesn't warrant our attention and our kind of, let's say, focus, but it's also not a one-to-one comparison.
zh: 这当然不意味着中国无能，当然也并不意味着它不值得我们的关注和我们的，比如说，关注，但这也不是一对一的比较。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think we should be very careful in implying it's a one-to-one comparison.
zh: 我认为我们应该非常小心地暗示它是一个一对一的比较。

speaker: Unknown
en: And by the way, that five-minute explanation is never going to get to the average person sitting at an airport in Detroit, Michigan waiting for their flight.
zh: 顺便说一下，那五分钟的解释永远不会传到在密歇根州底特律机场等待航班的普通人的耳朵里。

speaker: Unknown
en: All they consume is China bad.
zh: 他们消费的全是中国坏。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not like that.
zh: 并不是那样的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not that simple.
zh: 并没有那么简单。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's way more nuanced.
zh: 它要微妙得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: This episode is brought to you by Lovable.
zh: 本节目由 Lovable 赞助。

speaker: Unknown
en: Not only are they the fastest growing company in history, I use it regularly and I could not recommend it more highly.
zh: 他们不仅是历史上增长最快的公司，我经常使用它，我不能更强烈地推荐它了。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you've ever had an idea for an app but didn't know where to start, Lovable is for you.
zh: 如果你曾经有过一个应用程序的想法，但不知道从哪里开始，Lovable 适合你。

speaker: Unknown
en: Lovable lets you build working apps and websites by simply chatting with AI.
zh: Lovable 让你只需通过与 AI 聊天就能构建可工作的应用程序和网站。

speaker: Unknown
en: Then you can customize it at automations and deploy it to a live domain.
zh: 然后你可以在自动化方面自定义它，并将其部署到实时域。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's perfect for marketers, spinning up tools, product managers, prototyping new ideas, and founders launching their next business.
zh: 它非常适合营销人员、开发工具、产品经理、原型化新想法以及创始人们推出他们的下一个业务。

speaker: Unknown
en: Unlike no-code tools, Lovable isn't about static pages.
zh: 与无代码工具不同，Lovable 不是关于静态页面的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It builds full apps with real functionality and it's fast.
zh: 它构建具有真实功能的完整应用程序，而且它很快。

speaker: Unknown
en: We used to take weeks, months, or years, you can now do over a weekend.
zh: 我们过去需要几周、几个月或几年，你现在可以在一个周末内完成。

speaker: Unknown
en: So if you've been sitting on an idea, now is the time to bring it to life.
zh: 所以如果你一直有一个想法，现在是让它成为现实的时候了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Get started for free at lovable.dev.
zh: 在 lovable.dev 免费开始使用。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's lovable.dev.
zh: 那就是 lovable.dev。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you decided to join Twitter recently, put out your first tweet.
zh: 所以你最近决定加入 Twitter，发出了你的第一条推文。

speaker: Unknown
en: Your first tweet was just like, "Hello, I'm going to start tweeting."
zh: 你的第一条推文就像是，“你好，我要开始发推了。”

speaker: Unknown
en: That tweet got two million views.
zh: 那条推文获得了两百万的浏览量。

speaker: Unknown
en: Elon replied to you, Marc Andreessen quote tweeted it and said, "This is the best AI CEO nobody knows, follow for free alpha."
zh: Elon 回复了你，Marc Andreessen 引用推文并说，“这是没人知道的最棒 AI CEO，关注以获取免费 alpha。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Elad Gill, famed investor, describes you as the most successful, most quiet company in AI.
zh: Elad Gill，著名的投资人，将你描述为 AI 领域最成功、最低调的公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: And to me, this is really interesting because most founders are told, "Build in public, build a following, be loud, get out there, talk all the time about what you're doing."
zh: 对我来说，这真的很有趣，因为大多数创始人都被告知，“公开构建，建立追随者，大声喧哗，走出去，一直谈论你在做什么。”

speaker: Unknown
en: You did the opposite.
zh: 你做了相反的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: You were very under the radar, stay quiet, build, build, build, and then decided later, "Okay, now it's time to talk about our story."
zh: 你非常低调，保持安静，构建、构建、构建，然后后来决定，“好吧，现在是时候谈论我们的故事了。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think this counter narrative is really interesting and I think will inspire a lot of founders to not feel like they have to do this.
zh: 所以我认为这种反向叙事真的很有趣，我认为它将激励很多创始人不必觉得他们必须这样做。

speaker: Unknown
en: What was your philosophy of just staying quiet and then starting to be public?
zh: 你只是保持安静然后开始公开亮相的哲学是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, it's a great point.
zh: 是的，这很棒的观点。

speaker: Unknown
en: So number one, it was intentional.
zh: 所以第一，这是有意的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think if it was up to me, we would do that forever.
zh: 我认为如果由我决定，我们会永远这样做。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think we're very much inspired by folks more like a Berkshire Hathaway and less like, let's say, a Silicon Valley darling.
zh: 我们受到像 Berkshire Hathaway 这样的人的启发，而不是像硅谷宠儿那样的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I'll tell why I changed the views and then just before some founders go and take that advice immediately without really thinking about it, I can do that because I'm known in the ecosystem.
zh: 我会告诉你为什么改变了看法，然后就在一些创始人在没有真正思考的情况下立即采纳那个建议之前，我可以这样做是因为我在这个生态系统中很有名。

speaker: Unknown
en: I know these folks personally, and so I don't need to have a brand out there that is getting Elad to remember me and think about me.
zh: 我私下认识这些人，所以我不需要在那里建立品牌让 Elad 记住我并想到我。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so if I'm doing my first two companies, we're a lot less known is before I really came to YC.
zh: 所以如果我是在做我的前两家公司，我们在 YC 之前知名度要低很多。

speaker: Unknown
en: So all of our company values can be reduced to these two words of radical pragmatism.
zh: 所以我们所有的公司价值观都可以归结为这两个词：激进实用主义。

speaker: Unknown
en: So before you take the advice, make sure it applies to your situation.
zh: 所以在采纳建议之前，确保它适用于你的情况。

speaker: Unknown
en: One of the reasons, Naval, who's one of our investors and a friend, says fame itself is like a tool and it's powerful.
zh: 原因之一是，Naval，他是我们的投资者之一也是朋友，说名声本身就像一个工具，它很强大。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, if you don't have a network and you can get a following, that's a fantastic way to recruit people to your company, to recruit investors, to your mission, and then of course the customers.
zh: 现在，如果你没有网络并且你可以获得追随者，那是招募人员加入你的公司、招募投资者加入你的使命、当然还有客户的好方法。

speaker: Unknown
en: But for us, and I think that wasn't a hard requirement, 10 plus years ago.
zh: 但对我们来说，我认为这不是一个硬性要求，那是 10 多年前的事了。

speaker: Unknown
en: The other thing is I think Peter and I, the old saying about life is kind of like you do things and then you rationalize the thing that you do.
zh: 另一件事是，我认为 Peter 和我，关于生活的老话有点像你先做事情，然后你为你做的事情找理由。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think fundamentally Peter and I, we don't get a lot of, my co-founder, Peter Ludwig, we don't get a lot of emotional satisfaction out of doing very public things.
zh: 所以我认为根本上 Peter 和我，我的联合创始人 Peter Ludwig，我们从做非常公开的事情中得不到很多情感满足。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think if I was really to play armchair psychologist and really try to get to the root of why, beyond the rational view, which is focus on your customers, focus on the product.
zh: 我认为如果我真的要充当安乐椅心理学家并真正试图找出根本原因，超越理性的观点，即专注于你的客户，专注于你的产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: Every minute you're doing a podcast, every minute you're doing an X post, every minute you're writing something for public consumption, you're not focusing your very limited time that you have on your customers and your product.
zh: 你做播客的每一分钟，你做 X 帖子的每一分钟，你为公共消费撰写东西的每一分钟，你都没有把你有非常有限的时间集中在你的客户和你的产品上。

speaker: Unknown
en: And ultimately, that's the only thing that's going to produce and yield results.
zh: 归根结底，这是唯一能产生并带来结果的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the reality of the situation today in 2026 is even a company like us that's known or somebody like me is known in the ecosystem, you still want to get that broader message out.
zh: 但 2026 年今天的现实是，即使是像我们这样在生态系统中知名的公司或像我这样的人，你仍然希望把更广泛的信息传达出去。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's what I talk a little bit about on X. So it is definitely contrarian, but it's not just contrarian for contrarian's sake.
zh: 这就是我在 X 上谈论的一点。所以这绝对是逆向的，但不仅仅是为了逆向而逆向。

speaker: Unknown
en: It plays a little bit of our own psychology.
zh: 它有点符合我们自己的心理。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then I would say just to finish that thought there is I grew up, I'm an immigrant.
zh: 然后我想说只是为了完成那个想法，我长大了，我是一个移民。

speaker: Unknown
en: I came to the US from Pakistan when I was a kid, have a little bit of a weird name and you feel like anybody... I grew up in Detroit and Warren, Michigan specifically for all those at home.
zh: 我小时候从巴基斯坦来到美国，有一个有点奇怪的名字，你会觉得任何人……我底特律长大的，具体是密歇根州的 Warren，给家里的各位说。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when you feel that you're a little bit on the edge of society or you're not maybe in the mainstream, and this resonates with some people, that's not as resonant with everybody, you feel very skeptical of the mainstream because you're just on the outside for so long.
zh: 当你觉得你有点处于社会的边缘，或者你可能不在主流之中，这与一些人产生共鸣，但不是与每个人都产生共鸣，你会对主流非常怀疑，因为你在外面太久了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think you can trace a bunch of founders' psychology to this feeling of being an outcast actually.
zh: 我认为你可以将许多创始人的心理追溯到这种实际上是局外人的感觉。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so then you find yourself in a situation where you're like the COO of YC.
zh: 然后你发现自己处于一种情况，你是 YC 的 COO。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the narrative of I'm an outsider is like, I don't know if there's anything more inside than being the YC COO.
zh: 而我是局外人的叙事就像，我不知道有没有比成为 YC COO 更内部的事情了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think that reconciliation, I think over my career also has had to happen, which is maybe that's just kind of a weird kind of thing.
zh: 所以我认为这种和解，我认为在我的职业生涯中也必须发生，这可能只是一种有点奇怪的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so when I talked to Marc Andreessen who really pushed me to go online or Elad or whoever it is, their view is leave your baggage and your trauma in the background and really let's think more pragmatically.
zh: 所以当我和真正推动我上线的 Marc Andreessen 或 Elad 或其他人交谈时，他们的观点是把你的包袱和创伤留在背景中，让我们更务实地思考。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the pragmatic thing here is whether I like to do these types of things or not, fundamentally it helps get the message out.
zh: 这里的务实事情是，不管我喜欢做这类事情与否，从根本上说它有助于传达信息。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the message can be something very small and myopic like what's happening in physical AI and machines become intelligent or much larger, which is what's happening in society through this fundamental change that we're going through.
zh: 信息可以是非常小和短视的，比如物理 AI 中发生的事情和机器变得智能，或者大得多，这是我们通过正在经历的根本性变革在社会中发生的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've had the rare privilege or the experience of seeing the full economic spectrum.
zh: 我拥有罕见的特权或经历，看到了完整的经济光谱。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've really seen the extreme ends of both sides and truly, I really mean that.
zh: 我真的看到了双方极端的尽头，真的，我真的那个意思。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so somebody like Marc, who's close to our company says, "Well, those are some ideas that are worth getting out beyond just they're promoting whatever your company or something like that."
zh: 所以像 Marc 这样接近我们公司的人说，“嗯，这些想法值得传播出去，不仅仅是他们在推广你的公司或类似的东西。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And that I can get behind, which is like the debate and discussion about ideas and what's happening to our society because of these technical changes.
zh: 我可以支持这一点，也就是关于想法的辩论和讨论，以及因为这些技术变化我们的社会正在发生什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so here I am.
zh: 所以我在这里。

speaker: Unknown
en: Amazing.
zh: 太棒了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there's a few threads I want to follow there.
zh: 所以我想跟进几个线索。

speaker: Unknown
en: One is you were, as you said, COO at YCombinator, you saw a lot of startups up close.
zh: 一个是你正如你所说，你是 YCombinator 的 COO，你近距离看到了很多初创公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is your third startup on your own.
zh: 这是你自己做的第三家初创公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: Something that I hear you talk about is that successful companies almost always show traction very early.
zh: 我听到你谈论的一点是,成功的公司几乎总是很早就显示出发展势头。

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of founders here are like, "No, just keep trying fighting and maybe we'll be the next Figma and Notion four years in.
zh: 这里的很多创始人都说,"不,只要继续尝试和奋斗,也许我们四年后会成为下一个 Figma 和 Notion。

speaker: Unknown
en: We'll figure it out."
zh: 我们会弄明白的。"

speaker: Unknown
en: What's your experience there and what's your advice to founders who aren't seeing traction early?
zh: 你在那方面的经验是什么,你对那些没有看到早期势头的创始人有什么建议?

speaker: Unknown
en: Nuances.
zh: 细微差别。

speaker: Unknown
en: If I was starting another company, I'd call it Nuance.
zh: 如果我要再开一家公司,我会叫它 Nuance。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think what you're saying is correct.
zh: 所以我认为你说的很正确。

speaker: Unknown
en: I continue to believe that.
zh: 我一直相信这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think good companies tend to have traction fairly early and then just sustain it for a decade plus.
zh: 我认为好的公司往往很早就获得发展势头,然后将其维持十年以上。

speaker: Unknown
en: To the founders that's toiling, let's say if you're listening and you're about two years into your company and you're maybe having a tough time getting money and building that first product that consumers or businesses really love either through retention or dollars, two years is the difficult time.
zh: 对于那些正在苦苦挣扎的创始人,假设你在听节目,你的公司大约已经两年了,你可能很难融到钱,也很难构建出消费者或企业真正喜爱的第一款产品,无论是通过留存率还是收入来看,两年是一个困难的时期。

speaker: Unknown
en: The heuristic that I would use is if the information I'm getting from the market is not informing me on a more and more specific path, I would consider resetting.
zh: 我会使用的启发式方法是,如果我从市场上得到的信息没有给我指出一条越来越具体的路径,我会考虑重置。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what I mean by reset is oftentimes, and this is wearing my YC hat, seeing hundreds and thousands of companies, is oftentimes it's like the co-founding, literally the foundation upon which the house is built is not correct.
zh: 我所说的重置通常是,这是戴着我的 YC 帽子看到的成千上万家公司,往往是联合创始关系,也就是房子建立在地基上的那个基础,是不正确的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's like, imagine you built this house and every time you put a cup of water and it slides off the table and it falls on the ground and you keep adjusting the table, it's like maybe the foundation is actually wrong.
zh: 这就好比,想象一下你建了这所房子,每次你放一杯水在桌子上,它都会滑下来掉在地上,而你一直在调整桌子,这就像是地基实际上是错的。

speaker: Unknown
en: The whole house is off kilter.
zh: 整个房子都是歪的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the foundation might not only be your co-founders, it could be the market that you're in.
zh: 而地基可能不仅仅是你的联合创始人,也可能是你所处的市场。

speaker: Unknown
en: It could be the phase of life that you're in and the amount of effort that you're willing to put into that thing in order to make it successful.
zh: 也可能是你的人生阶段和你愿意为这件事投入的努力程度,以使其成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a bunch of reasons that a company can fail and you have to be able to somehow say, "I don't know what is the reason.
zh: 公司失败有一堆原因,你必须能够某种程度上说,"我不知道原因是什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm just going to have to have a hard reset here."
zh: 我只是必须在这里来一次硬重置。"

speaker: Unknown
en: One thing I would tell founders, and I tell Applied is creating a founder class in itself, people who've worked at Applied Intuition are now starting their own companies.
zh: 我想告诉创始人的一件事,也是我告诉 Applied 的是,它创造了一个创始人阶层本身,在 Applied Intuition 工作的人现在正在创办他们自己的公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: We have a thousand plus engineers and over time they're starting their own firms.
zh: 我们有一千多名工程师,随着时间的推移,他们正在创办自己的公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I say to all of them is, just imagine the first time you're going to do a startup for the first three year, it's a zero.
zh: 我对他们所有人说,想象一下你第一次要做初创企业的前三年,它是零。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just rid yourself of the expectation that it's going to be successful and that you're a craftsperson.
zh: 只是让自己摆脱它会成功的期望,而且你是一名工匠。

speaker: Unknown
en: If this was a woodworking podcast and you said the first table that you built was wobbly, you wouldn't say, "Well, go work at Crate & Barrel."
zh: 如果这是一个木工播客,你说你造的第一张桌子是摇晃的,你不会说,"好吧,去 Crate & Barrel 工作吧。"

speaker: Unknown
en: You'd say, "Oh, that's the first table.
zh: 你会说,"哦,那是第一张桌子。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're going to keep at it."
zh: 我们要继续做下去。"

speaker: Unknown
en: Being a founder is its own muscle and you want to exercise that muscle.
zh: 作为创始人本身就是一种肌肉,你想锻炼那块肌肉。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think a lot of founders, especially early in their founding career, put such an incredible pressure on themselves to make it great out of the gate that they actually miss the thing that you're getting in that first round, which is learning and building that muscle.
zh: 但我认为很多创始人,尤其是在他们创业生涯的早期,给自己施加了巨大的压力,要求一开门就很成功,以至于他们实际上错过了你在第一轮中得到的东西,那就是学习和建立那块肌肉。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the second, third time.
zh: 以及第二轮、第三轮。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think it's not random that my third company is the most successful company.
zh: 我认为我的第三家公司是最成功的公司,这并非随机。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that you see that more often than not.
zh: 我认为你会发现这种情况比不发生的要多。

speaker: Unknown
en: There are funds which are almost exclusively focused on multi-time founders for this reason.
zh: 有一些基金几乎完全专注于多次创业者,原因就在于此。

speaker: Unknown
en: What I love about that advice is often the best ideas come from when you have low expectations, you're just playing around, you're just tinkering, you're not like, I'm going to build the next great, I don't know, Google, it's just you having fun.
zh: 我喜欢这个建议的地方在于,往往最好的想法来自于当你期望值很低的时候,你只是在玩,你只是在摆弄,你不是在想,我要建立下一个伟大的,我不知道,Google,这只是你在找乐子。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's how I found this world that I'm in right now, this path.
zh: 这就是我找到我现在所处的这个世界,这条路的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: And OpenClaw is a good example of that.
zh: OpenClaw 就是一个很好的例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think why that advice is so difficult is if you hear this and you're in the proverbial war, you're like, "What the hell are these people talking about having fun?"
zh: 我认为为什么这个建议这么难,是因为如果你听到这个并且你正处于所谓的战争中,你会想,"这些人在说找乐子到底是什么意思?"

speaker: Unknown
en: I think this is hard.
zh: 我认为这很难。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so you have to hold these contrasting kind of or conflicting views in your head, which is like it's deeply very, very important and you should give it your all and it's also not that important.
zh: 所以你必须在你的头脑中保持这些对比的或冲突的观点,就像它非常非常地重要,你应该全力以赴,而且它也不是那么重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's a really hard thing to reconcile and keep in balance.
zh: 这是一个很难调和并保持平衡的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the way that you approached this company where you stayed quiet, that I think helps a lot where you're not-
zh: 而你创办这家公司的方式,你保持低调,我认为这很有帮助,你不是-

speaker: Unknown
en: Absolutely.
zh: 绝对的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Even at YC when I became COO, I told Sam Altman, was the president.
zh: 即使在 YC 当我成为 COO 时,我告诉 Sam Altman,他是总裁。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I told Sam, "Let's not announce this for a year because if the partners don't want me to be COO, it's not a successful thing.
zh: 我告诉 Sam,"我们一年内不要宣布这件事,因为如果合伙人不希望我做 COO,这就不是一件成功的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't have the pressure of the public scrutiny that why were you COO only for six months or something like that?"
zh: 我没有公众审视的压力,为什么你只做了六个月的 COO 之类的。"

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think you have to be very honest with yourself as a founder and as a human being that those things matter.
zh: 我认为作为创始人和作为一个人,你必须对自己非常诚实,这些事情很重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: What people think about you matter and it impacts yourself.
zh: 人们对你的看法很重要,它影响你自己。

speaker: Unknown
en: And having this spotlight on you.
zh: 还有聚光灯打在你身上。

speaker: Unknown
en: I always say it's very easy to pivot before you raise money and before you have employees.
zh: 我总是说,在你在你筹集资金和在你雇佣员工之前转型是非常容易的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Nobody cares.
zh: 没人在乎。

speaker: Unknown
en: The moment you raise money and more importantly, the moment you hire employees, employees joined a very specific mission.
zh: 当你筹集资金的时候,更重要的是,当你雇佣员工的时候,员工加入了一个非常具体的使命。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you go and you walk into the office and there's 10 of them, you say, "Guys, turns out this is wrong.
zh: 你走进办公室,那里有 10 个人,你说,"伙计们,结果发现这是错的。"

speaker: Unknown
en: We're going on a different mission."
zh: 我们正在执行不同的任务。

speaker: Unknown
en: Imagine if this was war.
zh: 想象一下如果这是战争。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, "What the hell?
zh: 这就像是，「这到底是怎么回事？

speaker: Unknown
en: We're attacking that hill and now we just say that hill's not important."
zh: 我们正在进攻那座山丘，而现在我们却说那座山丘不重要了。

speaker: Unknown
en: How do you know the next hill is important?
zh: 你怎么知道下一座山丘是重要的？

speaker: Unknown
en: And you as a lead, you lose a lot of credibility.
zh: 作为领导者，你会失去很多信誉。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's not only for the superficialness of being a credible leader, it's a practical nature of when you're very, very public, the startup becomes your identity, and then suddenly you're having to reconcile that actually that thing is not correct.
zh: 这不仅是为了成为一个可信的领导者的表面功夫，这是一种实际性质，当你非常非常公开时，初创公司就成了你的身份，然后突然你必须去协调那个东西实际上是不正确的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we have these core values in the company, and early in the company, I used to have this line which says, "Our best work is done alone and quietly."
zh: 所以我们在公司里有这些核心价值观，在公司早期，我曾经有一句话说，「我们最好的工作是独自安静地完成的。」

speaker: Unknown
en: And I deeply believe that.
zh: 我深深相信这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so founders, I would think of it that way, but it's for pragmatic reasons.
zh: 所以对于创始人来说，我会这样想，但这是出于务实的原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not like some, just because it's cool to be under the radar.
zh: 这并不是某种，仅仅因为在雷达之下工作很酷。

speaker: Unknown
en: It just allows you to maybe work in a bit more peace.
zh: 它只是让你也许能更平静地工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love these core values you've shared so far.
zh: 我喜欢你们目前分享的这些核心价值观。

speaker: Unknown
en: The last one, the best work is done alone quietly.
zh: 最后一条，最好的工作是独自安静地完成的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm so on board with that.
zh: 我非常赞同这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Radical pragmatism is the other one you shared earlier.
zh: 激进的实用主义是你之前分享的另一个。

speaker: Unknown
en: Are there a couple more there?
zh: 那里还有几个吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: These are gems.
zh: 这些都是珍宝。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Those are, I would say, the meta values.
zh: 那些是，我会说，元价值观。

speaker: Unknown
en: We have very specific, let's say, operating principles.
zh: 我们有非常具体的，比如说，操作原则。

speaker: Unknown
en: And this is real, as tactical as advice I can give to founders.
zh: 这是真实的，作为我能给创始人的战术性建议。
